summary: A full strip-and-reseal of a resilient/vinyl/PVC or sealed hard floor for a school or childcare site: isolate and protect the area, strip the old finish with caustic chemistry and a low-speed rotary, recover the slurry, neutralise-rinse and pH-check, dry fully, then build thin seal and finish coats before curing and an AS 4663 slip check. Every dilution, dwell, coat count and dry/cure/re-entry time comes from each product's SDS/TDS — never from a fixed rule.

steps.title: Confirm scope, book the window and pre-inspect the floor
steps.detail: Confirm the floor is a resilient/vinyl/PVC or sealed hard floor that is meant to be stripped and resealed (a genuine sealer/finish build-up), not a bare or no-wax floor that must never be stripped. Book the whole job into a term break, holiday deep-clean or after-hours window with the site. Identify the floor and its condition, measure the area in m², and cut a small test patch in an out-of-the-way spot to confirm the stripper lifts the finish cleanly. Critically, on older floors check the building's asbestos register first: pre-1990s vinyl tiles, sheet-vinyl backing and bituminous adhesives can contain asbestos, and stripping or machine abrasion would disturb it — if asbestos is present or suspected, stop and refer to a licensed assessor rather than abrading.

steps.title: Prepare documentation and plant before mobilising
steps.detail: Write a SWMS/JSEA for the caustic-stripping task. Build or confirm your hazardous chemicals register with a current SDS (no more than five years old) for the stripper, the prep/neutraliser, the sealer and the finish — for example Actichem Instastrip (SDS AP400) or Power Strip (SDS AP404), Pre-Seal/Preseal Pro (SDS AP171), Instaseal (SDS AP420) and Instagloss (SDS AP422). Read every SDS and TDS and plan the job backwards from them. Check the low-speed rotary and the wet vac each carry a current AS/NZS 3760 test-and-tag, and confirm workers hold the correct state or territory Working With Children check plus any site child-safe induction. (CCW/Actichem catalogue and 230 V plant availability checked 8 July 2026 — confirm current SDS/TDS and stock with the supplier before the job.)

steps.title: Move and protect furniture, then isolate and sign the area
steps.detail: Clear the room: move out furniture and loose items, and protect anything that stays — skirtings, door frames, fixtures and any adjacent carpet or surfaces — with masking and sheeting so caustic slurry cannot reach them. Hard-barricade the whole work area (physical barriers or a locked-off room) and put out wet-floor signage. In a childcare or school setting, physical isolation is the control that actually matters, because children may not read or heed a sign.

steps.title: Set up ventilation, power and spill control
steps.detail: Open up airflow — windows and fans — to move fumes away from any occupied part of the site, as the stripper's SDS directs. Power the rotary and the wet vac from a 230 V / 10 A GPO through an RCD/safety switch, and route leads clear of the wet zone so they are neither a trip hazard nor a shock risk. Set out your spill kit and clean flushing water, and decant the stripper only into a correctly labelled container — never leave an unlabelled bottle on a childcare site.

steps.title: Don PPE and do a final child-safety check
steps.detail: Put on the PPE the stripper's SDS specifies for caustic chemistry — splash goggles, chemical-resistant gloves (nitrile or neoprene), and a chemical-resistant apron and boots. Keep the Poisons Information Centre number (13 11 26) to hand and confirm you know the SDS first-aid steps for skin, eye and inhalation contact before you open the drum. Do a final check that no children or staff can enter the area.

steps.title: Dilute and apply the stripper at the correct dilution and dwell
steps.detail: Dilute the stripper to the ratio on the product label/SDS/TDS — no stronger 'to be sure', which only raises the hazard and the residue left behind. Use the TDS coverage rate (m²/L) to work out how much product the area needs. Apply an even, generous film to a section you can realistically strip before it dries, and let it dwell for the time the label specifies: dwell is what lets the chemistry break the finish down so the machine does not have to fight it. Keep the film wet for the whole dwell, and re-wet if it starts to dry.

steps.title: Agitate with a low-speed rotary/scrubber and stripping pad
steps.detail: Agitate the dwelled section with a low-speed single-disc rotary/scrubber — the low-speed class (typically around 175 rpm, not a high-speed burnisher) — fitted with an aggressive stripping pad (black or brown). Work in overlapping passes until the old finish is visibly emulsified into a slurry. Cut in edges, corners and under toe-kicks by hand with a doodlebug/edging pad or a stiff hand brush, because the machine cannot reach them. Work in manageable sections so no slurry dries back onto the floor before you pick it up.

steps.title: Pick up the slurry
steps.detail: Recover the emulsified slurry straight away with a wet vac or auto-scrubber before it dries — a dried slurry simply re-deposits the finish and you start over. Contain the slurry within the bunded/barriered zone. Inspect as you go: where old finish remains, re-apply stripper to that area, dwell, agitate and re-pick-up rather than leaving patches behind.

steps.title: Neutralise rinse and check pH (critical)
steps.detail: This step is non-negotiable. Flood-rinse the stripped floor with clean water plus a neutraliser/prep such as Actichem Pre-Seal/Preseal Pro (SDS AP171) to remove all stripper residue and bring the surface back to neutral, then recover the rinse. Any alkaline residue left behind will stop the new sealer bonding and can cloud or lift the finish. Check the result with a pH strip or meter on the damp floor or the recovered rinse water: it should read neutral (around pH 7), or the target the prep/finish TDS specifies. Repeat the rinse-and-recover until the pH is neutral and the water comes up clean.

steps.title: Inspect for residue and residual finish
steps.detail: With the floor clean and neutral, inspect the whole area in good light. Look and feel for streaks of leftover finish, cloudy residue or grit — check corners, edges and any seam or grout lines where finish hides. Re-strip and re-rinse any patch that is not clean and neutral. Do not seal over residue: it will telegraph through the new coats and undermine adhesion.

steps.title: Allow the floor to dry fully
steps.detail: The floor must be completely dry before any sealer goes down — sealing over trapped moisture clouds the finish and wrecks adhesion. Keep airflow moving. How long full drying takes depends on the floor, the coats to come and the conditions on the day, and any drying guidance lives in the TDS, so do not commit to a fixed figure. Confirm the surface is genuinely dry before you start sealing.

steps.title: Apply the first thin coat of seal
steps.detail: Apply the sealer base (for example Actichem Instaseal, SDS AP420) in a thin, even coat — thin coats dry and cure more predictably and build a more durable, even surface than one thick layer. Cut in the edges first with an applicator, then lay the body of the coat in slightly overlapping strokes, keeping a wet edge so laps do not show. Follow the TDS for how thin the coat should be and how many coats of seal it calls for.

steps.title: Observe tack-free/recoat timing between coats
steps.detail: Recoat only when the previous coat is tack-free — applying over a coat that is not ready traps solvent or water, clouds the finish and weakens adhesion. Test tack-free by touching an inconspicuous edge: it should feel dry, not sticky. The actual tack-free/recoat interval is product-specific and comes from the TDS, not from memory. Apply each further thin coat, alternating the application direction, and let each reach tack-free before the next goes down.

steps.title: Apply the finish/gloss coats and complete the edge-work
steps.detail: Build the presentable, protective surface with the finish/gloss coats (for example Actichem Instagloss, SDS AP422) over the seal, again in thin even coats — edges cut in first, then the body, keeping a maintained wet edge. The number of finish coats and the recoat timing come from the TDS. Between coats keep the area isolated, signed and free of all foot traffic so nothing marks the soft film.

steps.title: Cure and return-to-service timing
steps.detail: Tack-free is not cured. The finish keeps hardening after it is walkable, and full cure typically takes considerably longer than the recoat interval — the manufacturer's full-cure and re-entry guidance is in the TDS. Plan the job (coats plus drying between each, then full cure) backwards from the TDS and set the client's return date on that basis, never on a rule of thumb. Keep the floor isolated and signed for the whole curing period, and do not drag furniture back over an uncured finish.

steps.title: Slip-resistance check (AS 4663) and handover
steps.detail: A new finish can behave differently underfoot — especially when wet — so the job is not finished until slip resistance is verified. Arrange measurement of the finished, in-service floor in accordance with AS 4663 (which covers existing surfaces on site) and check it against the target slip class for that location. Take the target from the contract, SA HB 198 and the NCC — do not quote a fixed P-rating from memory. AS 4663 measures slip resistance only; it does not set re-entry times. Record the result with the job file.

steps.title: Dispose of slurry to trade-waste and restore the site
steps.detail: Contain and recover all stripping slurry and rinse water and dispose of it to trade-waste under the water authority's approval — never to a stormwater drain, gully, garden or play area. Once the floor is fully cured and slip-tested, remove the barriers, replace the furniture and hand the area back, confirming the reopening with the site contact.

steps.title: Record the job and close out
steps.detail: Complete the job record: products used with their SDS/TDS codes, the dilution and dwell applied, the coat counts and the recoat/cure guidance you followed, the pH verification for the neutralise rinse, the AS 4663 slip result against the target class, the trade-waste disposal reference, and before/after photos. On a school or childcare contract this documentation is as much what the client is buying as the finished floor.

safety: Strippers are high-alkaline/caustic (GHS corrosive) — read the product SDS (e.g. Actichem Instastrip AP400, Power Strip AP404) before opening; wear splash goggles, chemical-resistant gloves (nitrile/neoprene) and a chemical-resistant apron and boots, and ventilate as the SDS directs.

Keep the Poisons Information Centre number (13 11 26) to hand; know the SDS first-aid response for skin, eye, inhalation and ingestion before you start; have clean water ready for eye and skin flushing.
The stripped/wet floor and the freshly sealed floor are serious slip and chemical-contact hazards — hard-barricade and sign the whole area and keep it isolated for the full wet-and-curing period.
Power the rotary and wet vac from a 230 V / 10 A GPO through an RCD/safety switch; only use plant with a current AS/NZS 3760 test-and-tag; keep leads out of the wet zone.

Older resilient floors (pre-1990s vinyl tiles, sheet-vinyl backing, bituminous adhesive) may contain asbestos — check the site asbestos register before any aggressive stripping or abrasion; if ACM is present or suspected, stop and refer to a licensed assessor. Do not abrade.
Decant stripper only into a correctly labelled container; manage caustic slurry and spills per the SDS and never mix product chemistries.
childSafe: Schedule the whole job into a term break, holiday deep-clean or after-hours window — never around children.
Physical isolation beats a sign: use hard barriers or a locked-off room, because children may not read or heed signage.
Keep the high-alkaline stripper and every chemical secured and out of children's reach at all times; choose lower-tox prep and finish where the job allows and the TDS supports it.
Ventilate so fumes are kept away from any occupied part of the site.
Do not return the floor to service until it is fully cured AND slip-tested; coordinate the reopening with the centre.
Workers hold the correct state or territory Working With Children check plus any site child-safe induction.
Contain and recover all slurry and rinse water to trade-waste — never to stormwater, a gully or a garden where children play.
standards: Model WHS Act & Regulations (Safe Work Australia) as adopted by your state/territory; Victoria works under its own OHS Act & Regulations — check your regulator (SafeWork NSW / WorkSafe Victoria / Workplace Health and Safety Queensland).

Hazardous chemicals: current SDS (no more than five years old) for stripper, prep, sealer and finish; hazardous chemicals register (model WHS Regulation 346); correct labelling of decanted containers; GHS classification and pictograms.
AS/NZS 3760 in-service electrical test-and-tag on the low-speed rotary and wet vac (230 V / 10 A, RCD-protected).

AS 4586:2013 (slip resistance of new pedestrian surfaces — lab/pre-installation) and AS 4663:2013 (slip resistance of existing, in-service surfaces — the correct basis to re-verify the finished floor); recommended minimum classifications per SA HB 198 and the NCC — do not quote a fixed P-rating from memory.
National Quality Framework / National Quality Standard Quality Area 2 (children's health and safety), overseen by ACECQA, for childcare settings.

State/territory Working With Children checks; trade-waste approval from the water authority to discharge to sewer; the general environmental duty and state EPA requirements keep contaminated water out of stormwater.

Dilution, dwell, coat count, tack-free/recoat, full-cure and re-entry times are product-specific — follow each product's SDS/TDS, not a fixed rule. This is professional-development and contract-readiness training; it is not an IICRC-accredited course and awards no IICRC CECs.
documentation: SWMS/JSEA for the caustic-stripping task (and a Construction Induction / White Card if the managed site requires one for access).
Hazardous chemicals register with a current SDS for every product on site.
AS/NZS 3760 test-and-tag records for the rotary and wet vac.
Product and method record: products used with SDS/TDS codes, dilutions applied, dwell used, coat counts, and the manufacturer recoat/cure guidance followed.
pH verification record for the neutralise rinse.
AS 4663 slip-resistance result for the finished floor against the target class.
Trade-waste approval reference and slurry disposal record.
Reopening confirmation (fully cured and slip-tested) agreed with the site, plus before/after photos.
contentBodyHtml:
Service Procedure — Stripping & Sealing

A full strip-and-reseal of a resilient/vinyl/PVC or sealed hard floor is one of the highest-risk jobs in commercial floor care: it uses caustic chemistry, leaves a wet and slippery surface, and depends on getting a sequence of steps right in the right order. This procedure walks the job end to end for a school or childcare site. Every dilution, dwell, coat count and dry/cure/re-entry time is product-specific and comes from the product's Safety Data Sheet (SDS) and Technical Data Sheet (TDS) — never from a fixed rule or from memory.

Step-by-step procedure

  1. Confirm scope, book the window and pre-inspect the floor. Confirm the floor is a resilient/vinyl/PVC or sealed hard floor that is meant to be stripped and resealed, not a bare or no-wax floor that must never be stripped. Book the whole job into a term break, holiday deep-clean or after-hours window. Identify the floor and its condition, measure the area in m², and cut a small test patch in an out-of-the-way spot to confirm the stripper lifts the finish cleanly. On older floors, check the building's asbestos register first: pre-1990s vinyl tiles, sheet-vinyl backing and bituminous adhesives can contain asbestos, and stripping or machine abrasion would disturb it — if asbestos is present or suspected, stop and refer to a licensed assessor rather than abrading.
  2. Prepare documentation and plant before mobilising. Write a SWMS/JSEA for the caustic-stripping task. Build or confirm your hazardous chemicals register with a current SDS (no more than five years old) for the stripper, the prep/neutraliser, the sealer and the finish — for example Actichem Instastrip (SDS AP400) or Power Strip (SDS AP404), Pre-Seal/Preseal Pro (SDS AP171), Instaseal (SDS AP420) and Instagloss (SDS AP422). Read every SDS and TDS and plan the job backwards from them. Check the low-speed rotary and the wet vac each carry a current AS/NZS 3760 test-and-tag, and confirm workers hold the correct state or territory Working With Children check plus any site induction.
  3. Move and protect furniture, then isolate and sign the area. Move out furniture and loose items, and protect anything that stays — skirtings, door frames, fixtures and any adjacent carpet — with masking and sheeting so caustic slurry cannot reach them. Hard-barricade the whole work area (barriers or a locked-off room) and put out wet-floor signage. Physical isolation is the control that matters, because children may not read or heed a sign.
  4. Set up ventilation, power and spill control. Open up airflow — windows and fans — to move fumes away from any occupied part of the site, as the SDS directs. Power the rotary and the wet vac from a 230 V / 10 A GPO through an RCD/safety switch, and route leads clear of the wet zone. Set out your spill kit and clean flushing water, and decant the stripper only into a correctly labelled container.
  5. Don PPE and do a final child-safety check. Put on the PPE the stripper's SDS specifies for caustic chemistry — splash goggles, chemical-resistant gloves (nitrile or neoprene), and a chemical-resistant apron and boots. Keep the Poisons Information Centre number (13 11 26) to hand and confirm you know the SDS first-aid steps before you open the drum. Check that no children or staff can enter the area.
  6. Dilute and apply the stripper at the correct dilution and dwell. Dilute the stripper to the ratio on the product label/SDS/TDS — no stronger ‘to be sure’, which only raises the hazard and the residue. Use the TDS coverage rate (m²/L) to work out how much you need. Apply an even, generous film to a section you can realistically strip before it dries, and let it dwell for the time the label specifies. Keep the film wet for the whole dwell, and re-wet if it starts to dry.
  7. Agitate with a low-speed rotary/scrubber and stripping pad. Agitate the dwelled section with a low-speed single-disc rotary/scrubber (typically around 175 rpm, not a high-speed burnisher) fitted with an aggressive stripping pad (black or brown), until the old finish is visibly emulsified. Cut in edges, corners and under toe-kicks by hand with a doodlebug/edging pad or hand brush. Work in manageable sections so no slurry dries back onto the floor.
  8. Pick up the slurry. Recover the emulsified slurry straight away with a wet vac or auto-scrubber before it dries — a dried slurry re-deposits the finish and you start over. Contain the slurry within the barriered zone. Where old finish remains, re-apply stripper, dwell, agitate and re-pick-up rather than leaving patches.
  9. Neutralise rinse and check pH (critical). Flood-rinse the stripped floor with clean water plus a neutraliser/prep such as Actichem Pre-Seal/Preseal Pro (SDS AP171) to remove all stripper residue and bring the surface back to neutral, then recover the rinse. Any alkaline residue left behind will stop the new sealer bonding and can cloud or lift the finish. Check the result with a pH strip or meter: it should read neutral (around pH 7), or the target the prep/finish TDS specifies. Repeat the rinse-and-recover until the pH is neutral and the water comes up clean.
  10. Inspect for residue and residual finish. In good light, look and feel for streaks of leftover finish, cloudy residue or grit — check corners, edges and seam lines where finish hides. Re-strip and re-rinse any patch that is not clean and neutral. Do not seal over residue.
  11. Allow the floor to dry fully. The floor must be completely dry before any sealer goes down — sealing over trapped moisture clouds the finish and wrecks adhesion. Keep airflow moving. Drying time depends on the floor and the conditions and any guidance is in the TDS, so do not commit to a fixed figure. Confirm dryness before sealing.
  12. Apply the first thin coat of seal. Apply the sealer base (for example Actichem Instaseal, SDS AP420) in a thin, even coat — thin coats dry and cure more predictably and build a more durable surface than one thick layer. Cut in the edges first, then lay the body in slightly overlapping strokes, keeping a wet edge. Follow the TDS for coat thickness and the number of seal coats.
  13. Observe tack-free/recoat timing between coats. Recoat only when the previous coat is tack-free — over a coat that is not ready, you trap solvent or water, cloud the finish and weaken adhesion. Test tack-free by touching an inconspicuous edge. The recoat interval is product-specific and comes from the TDS. Apply each further thin coat, alternating direction, letting each reach tack-free first.
  14. Apply the finish/gloss coats and complete the edge-work. Build the presentable, protective surface with the finish/gloss coats (for example Actichem Instagloss, SDS AP422) over the seal, again in thin even coats — edges cut in first, then the body, keeping a wet edge. The number of finish coats and the recoat timing come from the TDS. Between coats, keep the area isolated, signed and free of all foot traffic.
  15. Cure and return-to-service timing. Tack-free is not cured. The finish keeps hardening after it is walkable, and full cure typically takes considerably longer than the recoat interval — the full-cure and re-entry guidance is in the TDS. Plan the job (coats plus drying, then full cure) backwards from the TDS and set the client's return date on that basis. Keep the floor isolated and signed for the whole curing period, and do not drag furniture back over an uncured finish.
  16. Slip-resistance check (AS 4663) and handover. A new finish can behave differently underfoot, so verify slip resistance before handover. Arrange measurement of the finished, in-service floor in accordance with AS 4663 and check it against the target slip class for that location (take the target from the contract, SA HB 198 and the NCC — do not quote a fixed P-rating from memory). AS 4663 measures slip resistance only; it does not set re-entry times. Record the result.
  17. Dispose of slurry to trade-waste and restore the site. Contain and recover all stripping slurry and rinse water and dispose of it to trade-waste under the water authority's approval — never to a stormwater drain, gully, garden or play area. Once the floor is fully cured and slip-tested, remove the barriers, replace the furniture and hand the area back, confirming the reopening with the site.
  18. Record the job and close out. Complete the job record: products used with SDS/TDS codes, dilution and dwell applied, coat counts and the recoat/cure guidance followed, the pH verification for the neutralise rinse, the AS 4663 slip result, the trade-waste disposal reference, and before/after photos.

Safety

  • Strippers are high-alkaline/caustic (GHS corrosive) — read the product SDS (e.g. Actichem Instastrip AP400, Power Strip AP404) before opening; wear splash goggles, chemical-resistant gloves (nitrile/neoprene), apron and boots, and ventilate as the SDS directs.
  • Keep the Poisons Information Centre number (13 11 26) to hand; know the SDS first-aid response for skin, eye, inhalation and ingestion; have clean water ready for flushing.
  • The stripped/wet floor and the freshly sealed floor are serious slip and chemical-contact hazards — hard-barricade and sign the area and keep it isolated for the full wet-and-curing period.
  • Power the rotary and wet vac from a 230 V / 10 A GPO through an RCD/safety switch; use only plant with a current AS/NZS 3760 test-and-tag; keep leads out of the wet zone.
  • Older resilient floors (pre-1990s vinyl tiles, sheet-vinyl backing, bituminous adhesive) may contain asbestos — check the site asbestos register before any aggressive stripping or abrasion; if ACM is present or suspected, stop and refer to a licensed assessor. Do not abrade.
  • Decant stripper only into a correctly labelled container; manage caustic slurry and spills per the SDS and never mix product chemistries.

Child-safe

  • Schedule the whole job into a term break, holiday deep-clean or after-hours window — never around children.
  • Physical isolation beats a sign: use hard barriers or a locked-off room, because children may not read or heed signage.
  • Keep the high-alkaline stripper and every chemical secured and out of children's reach; choose lower-tox prep and finish where the job allows and the TDS supports it.
  • Ventilate so fumes are kept away from any occupied part of the site.
  • Do not return the floor to service until it is fully cured AND slip-tested; coordinate the reopening with the centre.
  • Workers hold the correct state or territory Working With Children check plus any site child-safe induction.
  • Contain and recover all slurry and rinse water to trade-waste — never to stormwater, a gully or a garden where children play.

Standards & compliance

  • Model WHS Act & Regulations (Safe Work Australia) as adopted by your state/territory; Victoria works under its own OHS Act & Regulations — check your regulator (SafeWork NSW / WorkSafe Victoria / Workplace Health and Safety Queensland).
  • Hazardous chemicals: current SDS (no more than five years old) for stripper, prep, sealer and finish; hazardous chemicals register (model WHS Regulation 346); correct labelling of decanted containers; GHS classification and pictograms.
  • AS/NZS 3760 in-service electrical test-and-tag on the low-speed rotary and wet vac (230 V / 10 A, RCD-protected).
  • AS 4586:2013 (new pedestrian surfaces — lab/pre-installation) and AS 4663:2013 (existing, in-service surfaces — the correct basis to re-verify the finished floor); recommended minimum classifications per SA HB 198 and the NCC — do not quote a fixed P-rating from memory.
  • National Quality Framework / National Quality Standard Quality Area 2 (children's health and safety), overseen by ACECQA, for childcare settings; state/territory Working With Children checks.
  • Trade-waste approval from the water authority to discharge to sewer; the general environmental duty and state EPA requirements keep contaminated water out of stormwater.
  • Dilution, dwell, coat count, tack-free/recoat, full-cure and re-entry times are product-specific — follow each product's SDS/TDS, not a fixed rule. This is professional-development and contract-readiness training; it is not an IICRC-accredited course and awards no IICRC CECs.

Document this

  • SWMS/JSEA for the caustic-stripping task (and a Construction Induction / White Card if the managed site requires one for access).
  • Hazardous chemicals register with a current SDS for every product on site.
  • AS/NZS 3760 test-and-tag records for the rotary and wet vac.
  • Product and method record: products used with SDS/TDS codes, dilutions applied, dwell used, coat counts, and the manufacturer recoat/cure guidance followed.
  • pH verification record for the neutralise rinse.
  • AS 4663 slip-resistance result for the finished floor against the target class.
  • Trade-waste approval reference and slurry disposal record.
  • Reopening confirmation (fully cured and slip-tested) agreed with the site, plus before/after photos.
